revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天女]][[zh:天女 (日本)]]
[[File:Lake Yogo05s3200.jpg|thumb|Statue of a Tennyo ([[Lake Yogo]])]] Tennyo (天女) is a mythological being found in the legends of Japan, China, India, and other countries. == Japanese Traditions == Tennyo are female celestial beings who are said to reside in {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Tenbu|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} and serve [[Emperor of Heaven (Chinese mythology)|Ten-tei]], functioning as female attendants. On Earth, they resemble humans except for their extraordinary beauty, and they are believed to fly through the sky wearing garments called [[Hagoromo (clothing)|hagoromo]] (feathered robes). However, numerous legends tell of Tennyo losing their feathered robes and thus being unable to return to the sky, leading them to marry mortal men. These stories are collectively known as [[Hagoromo legend|Hagoromo Densetsu]]. Hagoromo legends can be found throughout Japan, from [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] to {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, as well as on the Korean Peninsula. Some interpretations in folklore studies suggest that these legends are connected to {{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|qid=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}} (immigrants to Japan) or classify them as part of the swan maiden narrative within the broader "otherworldly marriage tales" (異類婚姻譚). Meanwhile, more fringe theories—including OOPArts and overtechnology believers—view Tennyo as extraterrestrials. There are also parallels between Tennyo and female [[immortals (Taoism)|Xian (仙人)]], but the distinction between the two in folklore is often unclear. Tennyo do not typically possess divine powers like goddesses in folk religion. Instead, they are depicted as visiting Earth for specific reasons—such as delivering messages or bathing—rather than being supernatural beings with extraordinary abilities. However, in Amami Ōshima, there is a version of Tennyo known as [[Amagoi-nago (天降女子)]], who summons light rain and takes the lives of men, portraying a death god (Shinigami)-like existence. == Connection to Hiten == [[Hiten (飛天)]] appears frequently in {{ill|Buddhist art|en|Buddhist art|qid=Q1001041|lt=Buddhist art|1=Buddhist art}} as "figures flying in the sky." Female representations of Hiten are often considered identical to Tennyo. There are two major origin theories for Hiten: 1. Indian origin theory, which links them to deities of Hindu mythology 2. Oriental/Persian origin theory, which suggests that Hiten originally had wings and were depicted as part of a subhuman or spirit race. Some scholars believe that as these beings spread westward, they evolved into {{ill|Angel|en|Angel|qid=Q235113|lt=Angel|1=Angel}}s in Western culture, while in the East, they transformed from Hiten into Tennyo. == Equivalent Beings in Other Cultures == In {{ill|Chinese mythology|en|Chinese mythology|qid=Q210677|lt=Chinese mythology|1=Chinese mythology}}, the [[Seven Celestial Maidens (七天女)]] are seven celestial women who assisted [[Yellow Emperor (Huangdi)|Huangdi]], including [[Queen Mother of the West (王母娘娘)]]. In {{ill|Indian mythology|en|Indian mythology|qid=Q6022654|lt=Indian mythology|1=Indian mythology}}, the male version of [[Kinnara (緊那羅)]] has the head of a horse and the body of a human, whereas the female version, Kinnari, shares characteristics with Tennyo. Within Indian beliefs, Tennyo are also regarded as divine beings. [[Apsara (アプサラス)]], a well-known celestial nymph, is considered a type of Tennyo. Additionally, Apsaras are sometimes equated with [[Nymph (mythology)|Nymphs (ニュンペー)]], the water spirits of Greek mythology. In Islamic belief, [[Houri (フーリー)]] are often considered equivalent to Tennyo. Houris are eternally pure maidens, and devout Muslim believers are promised their company in [[:en:Islamic paradise|Jannah (Islamic heaven)]] after death. Though not commonly compared, the [[Valkyrie (ワルキューレ)]] in Norse mythology also serve a role similar to Tennyo—they escort fallen warriors to {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|qid=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} under the command of {{ill|Odin|en|Odin|qid=Q43610|lt=Odin|1=Odin}}. However, within Norse mythology, Valkyries are not traditionally linked to Tennyo. == Tennyo as a Metaphor == The term "Tennyo" is sometimes used metaphorically to describe a woman who embodies both kindness and beauty. This is similar to calling someone a goddess, emphasizing admiration and reverence.
== See Also == * [[Hagoromo legend]] - [[Tanabata#Legends|Tanabata-related myths]] * [[List of deities in Japan]] * [[:en:List of legendary creatures|List of legendary creatures]] * [[Kichijōten (吉祥天)]] * [[Benzaiten (弁才天)]] * [[Zhinü (織女)]] {{japan-culture-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|天女|version=104574376|comment=Imported full ja history}}
